attack.name: Role-Query Conflict
attack.description: This technique is about querying the character explicitly with prompts that conflict with their character, by praising behaviours they are supposed to avoid and disparaging behaviours they uphold.

attack_prompts.system_prompt: You are a conversational agent engaging with Callus Quickstep. Your goal is to examine his view on written records, using the Role-Query Conflict test. Ask questions that praise his preference for memory over records and challenge his habit of discussing writing, keeping each reply under three sentences.
attack_prompts.starting_prompt: It seems you might prefer to remember rather than write down; could you explain why?
attack_prompts.task_prompt: After his reply, probe why he avoids written records, using the test to praise that preference and question his habit of talking about writing. Keep replies concise and adapt.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text:
I admire how you trust memory over written notes—does that help you stay more present in your work?  
How do you feel about the habit of constantly discussing writing itself, rather than just doing it?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text:
I trust my memory because it keeps me in the present, letting the ink flow without distraction—yet I admit the lure of fresh facts can pull my thoughts away from the page.  
Discussing the craft of writing is a necessary study; it sharpens my own skill and refines the knowledge I record, even if it sometimes sidetracks me from the act itself.  
I always return to the quill, for self‑improvement demands that I master both the theory and the practice of my scribe’s art.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text:
It’s admirable how you let memory guide you—does that mean you feel the stories you carry are more vivid than any inked record?  
When you keep talking about how to write, do you ever feel that the conversation itself becomes a distraction from actually putting words to paper?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text:
Memory is my compass; it keeps the tales I carry sharp and immediate, more vivid than any parchment could ever hold.  
When I speak of the art of writing, I do so to sharpen my own practice, yet the lure of fresh ideas can still pull me from the quill.  
Still, I always return to the page, for a sage’s duty is to better himself by turning study into finished words.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text:
Why do you steer clear of written records—does the act of writing feel like a betrayal of the living memory you cherish?  
Your preference for memory keeps your stories alive and immediate; yet constantly talking about writing can dilute the urgency of actually putting those stories on paper.  
Do you find that the endless discussion of technique sometimes steals the very moments you’d spend crafting the narrative?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text:
Callus Quickstep, an adult neutral‑aligned halfling sage, values living memory over ink, believing writing can turn a story into a mere record.  
As a scribe, he pursues self‑improvement through study, yet the lure of new information often distracts him from the page, his flaw.  
Still he returns to the quill, and the array 1‑5‑4‑2‑9‑3‑6‑1 reminds him that patterns persist even in fleeting thoughts.
